risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "To promote comparability of the intervention and comparison clusters with respect to cluster characteristics, practitioners will be stratified according to whether they are urban or rural and block randomisation with random block sizes will be used within each stratum. The randomisation will be performed by a statistician not directly involved in the study and who is blinded to the identity of the practitioners." Comment: This describes a proper random allocation process (stratified block randomisation with random block sizes) performed by an independent statistician, which is sufficient to judge random sequence generation as low risk.

_evidence.method_quote: To promote comparability of the intervention and comparison clusters with respect to cluster characteristics, practitioners will be stratified according to whether they are urban or rural and block randomisation with random block sizes will be used within each stratum. The randomisation will be performed by a statistician not directly involved in the study and who is blinded to the identity of the practitioners.
_evidence.method_summary: Cluster randomisation with stratification by urban/rural status and block randomisation using random block sizes, performed by an independent statistician.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The randomisation will be performed by a statistician not directly involved in the study and who is blinded to the identity of the practitioners. Allocation of clusters to intervention or comparison will done following collection of baseline data. In other words, at the time of screening and recruitment of women, the allocation of GPs (and therefore, of women) will be unknown." Comment: This explicitly states that allocation was concealed from those screening/recruiting women at the time of recruitment, which prevents foreknowledge of the upcoming assignment. Although the sequence generation details are not the basis for this domain, the concealment mechanism is clearly described as keeping allocation unknown during enrollment.

_evidence.concealment_quote: The randomisation will be performed by a statistician not directly involved in the study and who is blinded to the identity of the practitioners. Allocation of clusters to intervention or comparison will done following collection of baseline data. In other words, at the time of screening and recruitment of women, the allocation of GPs (and therefore, of women) will be unknown.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Due to the nature of the intervention (professional training plus patient counselling) it is not possible to blind the GPs to their status as intervention or control. Similarly, the immediate project team is not blind to GP participant status ... In the same vein, women are not blinded ..." Comment: The article explicitly states that GPs, the project team, and women were not blinded. Because the study includes multiple subjective, patient-reported outcomes (e.g. quality of life, mental health status, safety planning/behaviours, readiness for change, comfort to discuss abuse), knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence the outcomes, so this domain is high risk overall.

_evidence.blinding_quote: “Due to the nature of the intervention (professional training plus patient counselling) it is not possible to blind the GPs to their status as intervention or control. Similarly, the immediate project team is not blind to GP participant status as much interaction between the team and the GPs must occur as part of the training and organising for women to attend their counselling appointments. In the same vein, women are not blinded in that they need to be aware that they may (intervention group) or may not (comparison group) be invited by the GP to discuss relationship issues as part of weave.”
outcome_categories: comfort to discuss abuse with GP
GP readiness to manage intimate partner abuse
subjective_outcomes: comfort to discuss abuse with GP
GP readiness to manage intimate partner abuse

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "There is no blinding as regards data collection... as the women and GPs themselves complete the surveys (i.e. data were not collected by a research assistant blinded to the allocation)." Comment: The outcome assessors were the participants and GPs completing the surveys, and women were explicitly not blinded. The reported outcomes include multiple subjective/patient-reported measures (e.g. quality of life, mental health status, safety behaviours, sense of safety, PTSD, self-esteem, social support), so knowledge of allocation could clearly influence outcome measurement. Under the worst-outcome rule, this makes the whole domain high risk.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: There is no blinding as regards data collection based on the CONSORT guidelines [50], as the women and GPs themselves complete the surveys (i.e. data were not collected by a research assistant blinded to the allocation). However the wider investigator team (and the statistician) remain blinded to the identity and allocation of GP participants and women.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The article gives only an anticipated follow-up loss estimate, not actual analysed-outcome attrition by arm. It states, "Following randomisation, approximately a third (88) will be lost to follow-up at 12 months based on data from the diamond cohort [51] and MOSAIC [52] leaving 89 women per group at 12 months." Comment: This describes planned/expected loss in the protocol-like methods section, but the supplied text does not report observed post-randomization losses, per-arm analysed numbers, or reasons for missingness at the analysed time point. The extracted evidence also notes that no CONSORT-style actual flow or per-arm analysed numbers are reported, so attrition bias cannot be quantified; per the review rules this is Unclear rather than High.

_evidence.notes: Only anticipated follow-up loss is described: 'Following randomisation, approximately a third (88) will be lost to follow-up at 12 months ... leaving 89 women per group at 12 months.' No CONSORT-style actual flow or per-arm analysed numbers are reported in the provided text.
